# Transcrição: 05-H-Scale Split - Duplicating Ad Sets for Scaling (Lower Reliability since 2020) 

- **Fonte:** 05-H-Scale Split - Duplicating Ad Sets for Scaling (Lower Reliability since 2020)

- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

This scaling strategy carries a level of risk and it's a good way of finding profitable sub-segments in your audiences and expanding on those in terms of scaling. So, H-scale split, horizontal scale split is based on taking segments of audiences that are working and separating those out into different campaigns, different ad sets and giving them their own budgets. So, for example, you might see that a particular age, gender, placement, et cetera is performing well. You might pull that out from your original campaign and your ad set, give it a separate budget and maybe go higher on that budget as well. It's a sideways scaling method. So, we're not pushing the budgets up necessarily on our original campaigns, but we're finding pockets of opportunities within those audiences. Now, before I show you those strategies, I want to give you what I call the Sharon Paradox. Now, the name is simply because as I was trying to picture this person in my mind going through this scenario, the name Sharon popped up. So, if you are a Sharon or if you know a Sharon, this is not based on you or your friend that you know. Now, the Sharon Paradox is this. Sharon shops online and let's say she's in there kind of 25 to 34 female demographic. She checks her mobile in the morning. First thing she likes to do is check out Instagram. So, she likes to be inspired or just see what her favorite brands are doing or all those people that she's following. She's a visual person. So, Instagram is her kind of natural place to go. Then she'll start her day, she'll get to work and she'll check out what's happening with her friends on Facebook. So, although Instagram is there for inspiration and just reading or visually reading some quick things, Facebook is where she goes to hang out with her friends and see what they're up to, et cetera. So, at lunchtime, she's browsing Facebook and then she might look at Instagram as well. And then kind of in the day, she may or may not be checking Facebook or Instagram. Then on the way home, she commutes. So, she's browsing Facebook on the way home just to see what her friends are up to. Maybe something going on in the evening. She wants to check it out as well. And then later on in that evening, she's on her laptop. Her mobile's next to her, but she's on her laptop whilst watching Netflix. So, this is a consumer journey in the sense of this is how this person's actually using their devices. So, here's the thing. She actually bought your product in the evening whilst she was watching Netflix. So, what was her journey like? Let's say, you know, this is what's happened. She saw your ad on Instagram in the morning.

## [03:01 - 06:01]

Although she noticed it, she didn't click or interact at that time. Then at lunchtime, she saw the Instagram story ad from your ad account as well. She actually watched the video and clicked into or swiped up just out of curiosity. Then later during her commute home, your DPA ad, your Dynamic Product ad kicked in. And it came up on Facebook. And although that she didn't interact with it, she saw it and it just reminded her of that product that she likes, but she didn't really go further on. But later on that evening, she actually typed your brand's name into Google. She found your brand, landed on the product that she saw earlier and she bought the product. And here is the paradox. If you had looked at your analytics, first of all, your analytics, if you're looking at Google Analytics, for example, would have told you that this person came through to your site through search. So they searched your brand. And you know, for all intents and purposes, it's a brand search. It may have even come through as direct, but more than likely you'll see your analytics as showing that it came through search. Now Facebook will have also claimed that sale as well. And you would have looked at that data and said, that story ad actually brought in the sale. What's the problem with this? Well, actually, there's a couple of problems. Although Facebook attributed the sale to Instagram stories, what about the impact of your DPA on Facebook? Because potentially without that, she may have actually forgotten about that product. So in this scenario, your dynamic product ad on Facebook is actually what convinced her to come back later to buy. And then she came back later to buy directly through a brand search. So if you look at this journey and you're a data driven marketer, you would look at this and clearly you'd say, Instagram story did its job. So we need to pull the placement out. We need to max our spend here. This is the way to go. You might look at Google Analytics and say, all right, I understand that a brand search was made. Google Analytics doesn't quite show me where it's coming from, but you know the brand search was made. And you know, you're running, for example, you might be running Facebook ads, Google ads, affiliate marketing, et cetera. So unless you go deeper into that and you know how to find the conversion paths, you might kind of dismiss that purchase from that was recorded in Google. And you'll say, you know, it's just a brand search. It's not really much we can make of kind of how to optimize that. But as far as Facebook's concerned, Instagram stories really work. So we're gonna really, really push our budgets up. So the first thing about this is, if you look at how this person actually bought, automatic placements would potentially make sense for you, right? Because if you're running an ad set and you're targeting all placements and letting Facebook decide, then Facebook will know Sharon's behavior.

## [06:01 - 09:04]

And here's the thing, Facebook understand, for any given user, they know what times you log in to your mobile, your desktop, what kind of apps you use at what time they know what times you're likely to click and browse and things like that. So, you know, I'd encourage you to actually map, just write down your own browsing behavior in a given day. And what I mean by that is, from the moment you wake up, make a note of which of the Facebook family of apps you're using, could be Facebook, it could be Messenger, it could be Instagram, for example, at what times you're using them and what you're using them for. So you are a sample size of one, but Facebook have that data. I just want you to understand that every interaction you're making on your mobile and desktop, Facebook is feeding into its profile engine. So when it looks at you as a, you know, one person, it knows that, right, you know, in the morning time, generally this person's on Instagram, spends five minutes, roughly a day, all that kind of stuff. All that data is held so that when you, as a potential user, enter the auction and Facebook says, what kind of ad should I deliver this person right now? Then Facebook will look at your behavior in the morning and say, this person has never bought in the morning. So therefore I'm more likely to try and serve an ad, which is, you know, for example, top of funnel, maybe a video, a visual or something like that. But it knows it's not, you're not gonna buy. So it's not gonna waste its time with, you know, retargeting ads or anything like that. And that's how the auction system works. So when you select automatic placements, the theory is that you're able to target the right person at the right time. Now, depending on your level of experience, you'll know whether this works for you or not. For me, it doesn't always work. For example, when I'm at the top of my funnel, I usually include Facebook and Instagram news feeds, may also include stories or I might pull that out into separate. One thing that Facebook try to do is allow you to use, you know, automatic placements, but to customize the creative for those different placements as well, which is something you should do. If, for example, you're targeting Instagram as a placement and you're using the same ad in stories as you are in the news feed, you're not gonna have the best experience because they are different types of usage. So automatic placements are great, but I feel that the creative execution is what lets people down. ## [09:04 - 12:06]

to find my good performers. So if you've been through the optimization stage of the training, you'll completely understand and know how to do this. So I'm not gonna repeat that here. And within that, you can find potential, profitable audiences. So that's all well and good. The next step then is identifying which of those audiences are a higher performers and then what to do next. So what I like to do is when I'm doing the H-Scale split strategy, my preference is to select unique segments. So this example in the right hand side is to illustrate what a unique segment looks like for me. So this is a broad campaign, for example. The broad campaign for me doesn't have a look alike, doesn't have an interest. It's just literally targeting, initially it was targeting a broad age group, 25 plus or female. However, what we found in the data was that different ages reacted to different creative, for example. So we split those placements out. So we've got 25 to 34 female, 35, 44, 45, 54, et cetera, as separate ad sets. So that was our first method of scale split strategy. Then what we did at that point was, if I'm running a CBO here, the budget is at the campaign level. If I see certain ad sets consistently performing well, I've got a few decisions to make. Do I let Facebook still push the budget where it thinks the overall performance is better or do I override that? And how I override that is a few methods. So first of all, let's say, for example, in this scenario, my 45 to 54 audience was severely underperforming, but there were some sales that were happening and I wanted to see how it would perform. I could, for example, take this out completely. So I could pause this one ad set and let the spend go into the other ad sets and scale up the spend that way. Or I could add a max spend at this ad set level so that spend would continue, but not as high as the other ad sets because I don't, although Facebook wants to speculate there, I don't want Facebook to spend the budget on the underperforming ad set. Yet I still want to run it because I'm doing grad testing. I may want to bring you ads in, for example. The other way is the opposite of this. So if, for example, in this scenario, I'm running these different ad sets, but I see a breakthrough performance for one of them. So 25 to 34 is performing absolutely amazingly well, but these ones are doing okay. You know, for example, let's say our target ROAS is minimum two, and I'm getting two, 2.4, 2.3, for example, on these three ad sets. But this one, I'm getting a ROAS of three, 3.5, for example.

## [12:06 - 15:07]

So, you know, your question there is, what do I do? I would take that into a separate campaign completely and I would go in with higher budgets on that as well. So that's kind of how I split out this particular strategy, which is to find segments that are profitable and I have confidence that the performance is good. Confidence for me is that it's been running for at least a week. So you're talking anywhere between five and 10 days. I'm getting a good number of conversions for that ad set. So again, you know, I'm looking at kind of 30 plus conversions per week for that one ad set that's running. And I want confidence that stability is already being reached. So the previous section talks heavily about stability and optimization. So I've gone through this process already. And let's say, for example, now the 25 to 34 age range is doing really well, then I'll pull it out into a separate campaign. But at that point, I have a decision to make. Do I still leave the original ad set running or not? If you leave it running, there will be auction overlap because at this point, I've got two separate campaigns running with the same audience. That causes auction overlap. So that's a risk. And it could mean that the new or the existing ad set could suffer in performance. Or actually the other thing to consider is pausing the original and letting the new one run. And there's risks and rewards of both because, you know, naturally you'd say, why would I pause an ad set that's performing so well? The reason is that it may be performing well now, but if you introduce, especially if you go higher budget. So let's say, for example, this current CBO campaign here, I'm spending $1,000 a day, but now I want to separate 25 to 34 and give that one audience itself $1,000 a day. Effectively, I'm doubling my spend. Do you understand that? I'm taking my existing campaign and by doing this, I can double my spend. So I'm pulling out my core audience. And, you know, for example, I'm going broad here. So the audience size is naturally big. And this is something which you need to bear in mind about the audience size is the bigger the audience size, the bigger you can duplicate and increase the budget for as well. So in this case, for example, I'll pull out the 25 to 34, put $1,000 in place, but I would absolutely make sure that I have my rules in place as well. Because this is the other thing about this is when you take a duplicate of a well-performing campaign or ad set and you go for a higher budget, then you need to make sure that you're monitoring it a lot closer. So a big part of the BPM method is about creating stability and optimization, about being able to scale up, but do it in a way that does not increase your stress as an advertiser. However, when you start applying things like this, faster scaling in particular, that creates an element of risk. And risk then causes anxiety and stress

## [15:07 - 18:08]

because you need to keep an eye on it. So just bear that in mind when you do go through this process, that you have to balance out the opportunity of scaling faster versus the cost of potential loss of stability. So as I've mentioned, I go for unique segments, potentially age and gender, which is easier because you're never gonna be more than one of those. So if you're falling to 25 to 34, that's all you are right now, unless you become 35 tomorrow, then that's fine, you move into the next segment. Gender, male, female, doesn't generally change. Country and region, now sometimes that will change and it depends on your targeting. So if you're a targetting everyone that lives in a particular location, you've got people flying in from different places, that can have an impact. But generally, country and region are stable. Placement and device, that's slightly different. Now, for me, when you're splitting out placement and device, it gets a bit more complicated. And my advice here is not split out this for scaling because it can create more instability than it can benefit. So for example, if you take your best audiences and you split your segment and you go into your reporting and you look at your data by platform device, for example, and you find that certain platforms or devices are working better. So for example, it could be that iOS users are more profitable, higher ROAS than Android, for example, you might naturally want to split that out. That's fine because generally an iPhone user and Android user are separate in terms of they're either one or the other. But where the problem occurs is if you're splitting desktop versus mobile, as I showed in the Sharon paradox, someone could be both in a single day. So as you know, I do often, I'm working on my laptop or browse Facebook, I might go on my mobile as well, at the same time even as well. So if you're splitting those campaigns out, it can create instability because you're overriding Facebook's own knowledge of how this particular user likes to interact as well. So going back to how I like to run ad accounts, I prefer fewer segments and fragments in my ad accounts. However, there is a time and place to consider doing this, especially if you see breakout performance and you want to balance your budget and kind of maintain performance, but also speculate on potential increase in budget, increase in performance as well. The other part of this is how you would potentially split angles and for example, this screenshot here on the right says DCO in the campaign name. For example, what I would do in this scenario, if the 25 to 34 age range worked really well, is not only would I duplicate that into its own CBO campaign, I would also make at least three copies

## [18:08 - 21:09]

of that same audience as well. So I'd have my campaign, I'd have my winning segment for example, so broad 25 to 34 female UK, I would duplicate that three times and then I would introduce a DCO, like I've done in this campaign here, Dynamic Content Optimized Ad, because what that allows me to do is play each ad set by angle. Now you could for example, one of the other strategies we use is just duplicate ads in each of those ad sets and see how the auction performs, but what you could do is if you have more than one angle that's working really well, is take your DCO and go through the DCO part of the training to understand how I use it. Take your DCO ad in your duplicated ad set, so you've got for example, three ad sets and a DCO in each of those and make sure each DCO has one angle only. So for example, if it's a fitness supplement for example, one angle could be weight loss, one angle could be weight building, one angle could just be feeling good inside for example. So those three angles, I would run in my separate ad sets. So that's another way to take your splits and go with higher budgets and then you might then balance your budgets across your different angles, but that allows you to spread risk because if there's one thing that I know, or I see a lot when it comes to scaling is, people will scale and they'll have one or maybe two creatives that performing really well and they'll scale it and scale it. The way I like to operate is that I'm always graduation testing in the background to bring new angles in, to bring new creative ideas, new copy, et cetera. So even if I have a winning ad and I'm scaling it to the moon and it does happen, sometimes you just have that one ad that's absolutely crushing it. Bring in the variants, start testing different headlines, different first few lines of the copy, different thumbnails, images, things like that because they can allow you to scale more consistently because when you are scaling and you run out of, your ads run out of steam, that's a problem because that causes massive issues as you start to scale up, you could take an ad set from a thousand a day to 10,000 to 50,000, but if that creative starts to fatigue, which means people have seen it too often, what do you do? Well, following the process of the BPM, you should have those ads already in place and ready to push into your high scaling ad sets as and when you need them. Now, the other thing about this is, keeping an eye on audience, auction overlap is really important because what you wanna do is if you are duplicating sideways in this method, and like I said in this kind of first point in the notes here, is think about unique segments because when those segments are unique, then you've got no chance of overlap. So for example, in this example here,

## [21:09 - 22:08]

my campaign is a broad CBO, but my ad sets are split by age. So for all intents and purposes, there'll be zero overlap here. If for example, I had two campaigns and in the other campaign I was running a lookalike with 25 to 34, there's a chance there'll be overlap. The overlap causes instability. So when you're using this strategy, do consider how unique your segments are. This strategy differs from the other H-scale strategy because of the way I split my segments. The other H-scale strategy as part of this section as well looks at, it's a higher risk strategy, but it doesn't split by unique segments as well. So this is a safer way of going sideways and scaling sideways and upwards by duplicating winning ad sets, your winning segments and increasing your budget, but it's still there to be used with caution.
